The highest pressure CIOs face overall is users requiring new function (Figure 1).
**Figure 1: Overall Pressures Requiring IT Budget Attention**
Oddly, the highest priority response is not focused on supporting new customer demand, as seen in Figure 2.
**Figure 2: Aggregate Prioritized Responses to Pressures**
By analyzing this apparent discontinuity in more depth, the data reveals a vast difference in IT budgeting approach between best-in-class firms and the remainder.
What is more striking is the shift in priorities among different populations as the data are sorted by best-in-class vs. the rest.
In best-in-class firms, the actions, capabilities, and enablers are more closely aligned with responding to customer demand for new or enhanced functions.
The difference is the degree of alignment within the IT organization: best-inclass IT organizations are distinctly effective in investing proportionately to support customer needs (Figure 3).
**Figure 3: Prioritized Pressures by Class of User**
Best-in-class firms prioritize the pressures they face quite differently from the rest.
While most identify customer requirements as the largest pressure, for best-in-class firms that is in third place, behind sustaining IT alignment and watching expenses.

This is not because Best in Class ignore customer requirements, but because they understand that by sustaining IT alignment they will be sure to meet the most crucial customer requirements as a normal part of doing business.
Also, the Best in Class see inadequate expertise (regarding IT budgeting) as a much lower priority concern than the rest.
This again is not because best in class don’t recognize this as a problem; rather, it is directly because best-in-class firms have solved that problem.
The differences between best-in-class firms and the rest appear across all industry sectors and all sizes of enterprise.
Best-in-class performers see the most significant challenge as sustaining alignment with business strategic priorities
• Best-in-class performers respond to the challenge by establishing customer-centric service levels based on business-relevant metrics

s shown in Table 2, survey respondents fell into one of three categories – Laggard, Industry Average, or Best in Class — based on their characteristics in four key A categories: (1) process (IT acquisition and lifecycle degree of standardization); (2) organization (degree of center-led purchasing governance); (3) knowledge (richness of build/buy/outsource strategy); and (4) technology (tools supporting consistent measurable governance).

Similarly, leading organizations generally deploy standards-based IT budget methods and govern the IT lifecycle consistently firm-wide (Figure 8).

Consistent, business-relevant service level metrics, financially designed to link business value to IT cost, are applied firmwide in nearly two-thirds of all best-in-class firms, and in no firms identified as laggards.

**Figure 9: Barriers to IT Budget Improvement**
In other words, the benefits of best-in-class performance are not restricted to the large ($1B or greater annual revenue) firms: all can benefit from process improvements.

Here the divergence between the laggards and the Best in Class is dramatic: laggards do not do continuous measurements, and best-in-class firms rarely use ad-hoc reporting techniques to understand their IT budgeting processes (Figure 10).

## **Best in Class Next Steps**
For the four fiscal quarters ended July 31, 2004, HP revenue totaled $78.4 billion.
High-tech and software manufacturers represented 12% of the sample, followed closely by education, which accounted for 11% of respondents, and computer equipment and peripherals, also 11%.
The public sector totaled 10% of respondents.

_**Company size** :_ About 16% of respondents were from large enterprises (annual revenues above US$1 billion); 21% were from midsize enterprises (annual revenues between $50 million and $1 billion); and 63% of respondents were from small businesses (annual revenues of $50 million or less).
